title: IRGC Missiles Hit Tankers in Hormuz as Ceasefire Frays, OPEC+ Floods Market
subhead: Iran's Revolutionary Guards fired missiles at commercial ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz, hitting a tanker and a second vessel and threatening the fragile US-Iran ceasefire — even as OPEC+ approved a fifth straight output hike, the UAE pumps unconstrained after quitting the cartel, and Saudi Arabia slashes prices by the most in two decades.

sections.heading: IRGC Missiles Strike Commercial Ships in Hormuz, Shattering Ceasefire Calm
sections.body:
Iran's Revolutionary Guards fired at least two missiles at commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz overnight Monday, according to two US officials cited by Axios. A tanker traveling south about 8 nautical miles east of Oman's Limah was hit on its port side by a projectile, sparking a fire on board, the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) reported. A second merchant ship was also struck by an Iranian missile, a US official told Axios. Both vessels sustained damage but no crew members were injured and no environmental impact was reported.

The attack followed the expiration of a week-long truce designed to halt hostilities in the strategic waterway, and came just weeks after Washington and Tehran inked a 14-point de-escalation pact in June. Indirect negotiations between Washington and Tehran in Doha last week concluded without breakthrough on the waterway's status, Axios reported. On Sunday, the IRGC sent naval patrol vessels to obstruct the so-called 'Omani route' through the strait, according to state media and an IRGC-linked Telegram channel. Tehran now demands that ship crews secure authorization to navigate channels cleared by its authorities.

Washington will probably retaliate by targeting Iranian assets, a US official said. CNN reported that almost 30% of ships transiting Hormuz between Friday and Sunday used the Omani coastal route, according to MarineTraffic data — 30 out of 108 vessels. MarineTraffic described activity as 'operational but fragmented.' Brent crude edged up 0.39% to $72.29/barrel; WTI gained 0.41% to $68.83.
sections.whyItMatters: The IRGC's missile strikes on commercial shipping are the most serious violation of the Hormuz ceasefire since it was signed — and they come just as tanker traffic was recovering. The move signals that Iran intends to maintain coercive control over the strait regardless of diplomatic agreements, and raises the probability of US retaliatory strikes.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Adds 188K BPD for August as Oil Surplus Fears Mount
sections.body:
The seven OPEC+ countries with voluntary production cuts — Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Algeria, and Oman — agreed on July 5 to raise output targets by 188,000 barrels per day starting in August, the fifth consecutive monthly increase. Saudi Arabia's required production rises to 10.416 million bpd and Russia's to 9.887 million bpd, according to OPEC's official statement.

The increases have been largely theoretical until now: the Strait of Hormuz closure during the Iran war kept actual output well below quota for Gulf producers. But with tanker traffic recovering since the June 17 ceasefire, these paper increases are beginning to translate into real barrels. Analysts at XS.com warn that global stockpiles may struggle to absorb the surge if the Strait stays open and flows remain uninterrupted.

Brent crude settled Monday at $71.99/barrel, down 14 cents on the day and nearly 40% below its March peak above $126. WTI settled at $68.55. The Brent futures curve tipped into contango last week for the first time this year — the six-month spread near minus 56 cents — signaling the market is paying for storage rather than scarcity. OPEC's own monthly report has trimmed 2026 demand growth in back-to-back months to under 1 million bpd.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is accelerating output into a softening demand environment, betting that market share matters more than price support. With the UAE now outside the quota system and pumping freely, the cartel's cohesion is under strain — and the oversupply signal is flashing even as Hormuz risk resurfaces.

sections.heading: UAE Pumps Near-Record Volumes After OPEC Exit, Sparking Price War Signals
sections.body:
The UAE raised crude output to above 3.8 million bpd in June, its highest level in over six years, after formally withdrawing from OPEC and OPEC+ on May 1. Energy Minister Suhail Al Mazrouei said the country's tens of billions in upstream investments — expanding capacity to 5 million bpd — meant it needed to maximize returns rather than keep production offline.

ADNOC shifted the official selling prices for its offshore Upper Zakum, Das, and Umm Lulu grades from a Murban-linked formula to the Dubai benchmark, aligning those medium-sour streams with competing regional crude. The company has also been offering discounted cargoes through tenders to expand its customer base. Meanwhile, Saudi Aramco set the official selling price for its flagship Arab Light crude to Asia at $1.50/bbl below the Oman/Dubai average for August — the biggest monthly cut since Reuters records began in 2003, a $1.10 drop from July.

Mizuho's Robert Yawger said the moves increasingly look like Gulf producers are gearing up for a price war. The UAE's unrestricted pumping and Saudi's aggressive price cuts are a one-two punch that could crater prices further if demand doesn't keep pace.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE's OPEC exit has removed the last restraint on Abu Dhabi's production ambitions. Combined with Saudi price cuts, this signals a shift from coordinated supply management to competitive market-share grabbing — a dynamic that could push Brent into the $60s or lower even as geopolitical risk persists.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Campaign Triggers Russia-Wide Fuel Crisis
sections.body:
Ukraine struck three Russian oil refineries and an oil terminal at the Baltic Sea port of Vysotsk on July 6, according to Ukraine's military and security service. Drone debris also damaged the ports of Ust-Luga — a major oil export outlet — and caused a power blackout in Sevastopol, Crimea. The strikes hit the Omsk refinery, Russia's largest, as well as facilities in Yaroslavl and Leningrad regions.

These attacks are the latest escalation in a sustained campaign that has pushed Russia into a nationwide fuel crisis. According to CNN analysis, nearly all of Russia's 83 regions are experiencing gasoline shortages or supply disruptions. The Norsi refinery, Russia's fourth-largest, stopped selling petrol and diesel on July 2 after a major unit was destroyed — the fifth Russian refinery to shut down. Repairs could take up to a year.

Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak acknowledged the crisis, saying Russia has enough fuel for domestic demand but that panic buying has increased consumption by 20-30%. Russia has slashed fuel quality standards and may suspend diesel exports. In Irkutsk, 5,000 km from Ukraine, authorities installed portable toilets at fuel stations because people were waiting for hours. One woman told the New York Times she waited 18 hours in a fuel queue with her 18-month-old child.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign has achieved what conventional military pressure couldn't: a genuine domestic energy crisis inside one of the world's largest oil producers. The disruption to Russia's refining capacity — and the potential suspension of diesel exports — could tighten global middle distillate markets even as crude oversupplies.

sections.heading: ADNOC Launches Global LNG Trading Platform as Gulf Producers Pivot to Gas
sections.body:
UAE state oil giant ADNOC launched a new global liquefied natural gas marketing and trading platform on July 6, based in the Abu Dhabi Global Market (ADGM). The platform consolidates the marketing and trading activities of ADNOC Gas and XRG with ADNOC Trading, targeting 47 million metric tons per annum in marketed volumes by 2035, according to Rigzone and WAM.

The move comes as the International Gas Union reported that global LNG trade hit a record in 2025, with strong US exports and rising European imports offsetting weaker Asian purchases. But the Middle East conflict continues to cloud the outlook — European aviation fuel supply has tightened to 80% of normal as Iran war disruptions cut imports.

ADNOC's platform launch signals Abu Dhabi's strategic pivot from purely oil-focused trading to an integrated gas proposition. The UAE already operates the Fujairah LNG import terminal and is developing export capacity at Ruwais, with first cargoes expected in 2028. The consolidation into a single trading entity mirrors the upstream integration strategy that ADNOC has pursued across its oil portfolio.
sections.whyItMatters: ADNOC's LNG platform is the Gulf's clearest signal yet that major producers are hedging their oil bets with gas. The 47 MTPA target by 2035 would make ADNOC one of the world's largest LNG marketers — and positions Abu Dhabi as a swing supplier between Atlantic and Pacific basin markets as LNG trade hits record volumes.

sections.heading: Japan's Rare Earth Crisis Deepens as China Keeps the Spigot Closed
sections.body:
Japan has begun extracting rare earth elements from discarded household air conditioners, Mitsubishi Electric leading the effort, as China's export controls cause shipments to Japan to remain down more than 80% year-on-year. Nomura Research Institute estimates the damage could reach $4.3 billion in lost Japanese production over three months, ballooning to $17 billion if restrictions persist for a full year.

Japan currently sources roughly 60-70% of its rare earth imports from China — already an improvement from nearly 90% dependency in 2010. But for heavy rare earths like dysprosium and terbium, which are critical for high-performance magnets in EV motors and wind turbines, Japan's reliance on China remains close to 100%. China imposed the export controls on January 6, 2026, specifically targeting Japanese entities and military-related applications — widely interpreted as retaliation for PM Takaichi's comments linking Japan's national security to potential Taiwan-related conflicts.

At the G7 summit, Takaichi pushed a mineral stockpiling plan, seeking to bridge divides on critical mineral supply chain resilience. Australia announced a $28 billion investment in critical minerals to bolster domestic processing capability. Meanwhile, the rare earth export controls China expanded in October 2025 continue to ripple through global markets eight months later, with 24/7 Wall St reporting that critical minerals ETFs are capturing the reshoring trade as China tightens controls.
sections.whyItMatters: China is weaponizing rare earth supply with surgical precision against Japan — the world's most exposed major economy. Japan's move to extract rare earths from air conditioner compressors is a vivid measure of desperation, not a solution. The 80% shipment collapse is a live demonstration of how critical mineral dependency can be leveraged in a geopolitical confrontation.

sections.heading: G7 Sanctions Target Shadow Fleet as Ukraine Strikes Tankers with Naval Drones
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in France, multiple leaders announced new sanctions targeting Russia's energy revenues. Canada imposed sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues, with PM Carney raising the issue alongside China EV tariffs in his meeting with Trump. The EU adopted a new sanctions package targeting Putin's confidants and Russia's prosecutor general, along with broader measures on energy revenues and propaganda. The UK announced new sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and seized a vessel in the English Channel — British Royal Marines boarded a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker.

Separately, Ukraine struck two shadow fleet tankers — the Gambian-flagged Kairos and Virat — in the Sea of Azov using Sea Baby naval drones. Both vessels were empty, heading to Novorossiysk to pick up cargo. The Kairos caught fire with critical damage; the Virat suffered minor damage. Crew members on shadow fleet vessels are reportedly paid in Bitcoin and stablecoins, revealing an extensive crypto-based sanctions evasion network.

The combination of Western naval interdiction and Ukrainian military action is squeezing the shadow fleet from both ends — though Russia has built redundancy into the system. CNN reported that the world has absorbed the loss of over a billion barrels of oil supply since the Iran war began with surprising ease, but buffer reserves are now drained and hedge funds have cut their net long position in Brent futures to near historical lows, according to Saxo Bank.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet is being attacked on two fronts — Western law enforcement at sea and Ukrainian military drones in port. The crypto payment revelation shows how deep the evasion architecture runs. But Russia's continued ability to move oil through these channels means sanctions impact is still more costly friction than effective blockade — and depleted strategic reserves mean less cushion for the next disruption.

bottomLine: The IRGC's missile strikes on commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz shattered the fragile ceasefire calm just as tanker traffic was recovering — a reminder that Iran intends to maintain coercive control over the waterway regardless of diplomatic agreements. The timing is brutal for oil markets: OPEC+ just approved its fifth straight monthly output hike, the UAE is pumping unconstrained after quitting the cartel, and Saudi Aramco cut prices by the most in two decades, all signaling a supply glut. Brent barely budged on the attack, closing at $72.29 — the market is treating the Hormuz disruption as a structural issue rather than an acute crisis, even as the IRGC physically blocks shipping lanes. Meanwhile, Japan's rare earth recycling from air conditioner parts reveals how painful China's export controls have become, Ukraine's drone campaign has created a genuine fuel emergency across Russia, and ADNOC's new LNG platform signals the Gulf's pivot from oil to gas dominance. The energy system enters the second half of 2026 with thinner buffers, fractured producer coordination, and new precedents for commodity coercion on both the maritime and mineral fronts.
sources: Axios, Reuters, Outlook India, Indian Express, UKMTO, CNBC, Rigzone, Financial Express, MSN, BusinessWorld, CryptoBriefing, CNN, FXStreet, Saxo Bank, Mizuho, Nomura Research Institute, WAM, 24/7 Wall St, MarineTraffic, Weekly Blitz, GivTrade, XS.com
